title: Bradycardia — symptomatic
oneLiner: HR <50-60 with hypotension, syncope, or pre-syncope — risk of asystole; common in sick sinus, heart block, drug toxicity, or acute MI.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Complete heart block (CHB) with escape rhythm failure:** wide QRS + ventricular rate <30 + hypotension → imminent arrest. **Finding:** P-waves not related to QRS, slow wide-complex rhythm.
- **Sinus arrest / ventricular standstill:** prolonged pause (>3 seconds) on monitor → syncope or seizure. **Finding:** flatline on ECG strip with no escape beat for >3 seconds.
sections.Presentation:
- • Dizziness, presyncope, near-syncope, or syncope (often with exertion)
- • Fatigue, exertional dyspnoea, confusion (in elderly)
- • Chest pain (if ischaemic cause)
- • Palpitations (if bradycardia with underlying tachycardia — ‘tachy-brady syndrome’)

**Key exam findings:**
- • Slow radial pulse (usually <50 bpm if symptomatic)
- • Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) — sign of haemodynamic instability
- • Cool peripheries, reduced cap refill, oliguria
- • Signs of heart failure: raised JVP, peripheral oedema, basal crackles
- • Carotid bruit (if vascular cause)
- • Assess for signs of hypothyroidism: coarse skin, delayed reflexes, gotre
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sinus bradycardia (physiological or pathological):** regular rhythm, P-wave before each QRS, often rate 40-60. Common in athletes, young adults.
- **First-degree AV block:** PR interval >200ms. Usually asymptomatic — rarely cause of syncope.
- **Second-degree AV block Mobitz I (Wenckebach):** progressive PR lengthening then dropped QRS. Usually benign unless very slow.
- **Second-degree AV block Mobitz II:** constant PR, then dropped QRS — risk of progression to CHB. Always consider ischaemia.
- **Complete heart block:** P-waves marching through, QRS independently slow (narrow or wide). Requires monitoring for pacing.
- **Sick sinus syndrome:** alternating bradycardia (often <40) with episodes of atrial tachycardia; pause after termination of tachycardia.
- **Drug-induced:** beta-blockers, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin, amiodarone, lithium, clonidine, methyldopa.
- **Hypothyroidism:** bradycardia + cold intolerance + weight gain + dry skin.
- **Hypothermia:** core temp <35°C + bradycardia (Osborn J waves on ECG).
- **Raised intracranial pressure:** Cushing reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing).

**Splitter:** Narrow QRS vs wide QRS escape rhythm in bradycardia. Wide QRS (especially >120ms) suggests infranodal block (Mobitz II, CHB) — higher risk of asystole — needs urgent pacing. Narrow QRS suggests nodal disease (sinus node, AV node) — often more amenable to atropine.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - • Immediate 12-lead ECG — document rhythm, PR interval, QRS width, ischaemic changes.
  - • Continuous cardiac monitoring — look for pauses, progression to CHB.
  - • Blood pressure every 5 minutes (if unstable).
  - • Capillary glucose — exclude hypoglycaemia as cause of collapse.
- **Bloods:**
  - • Routine: FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, troponin, thyroid function (TSH), digoxin level (if on digoxin).
  - • Consider: drug screen (if suspected toxicity), cortisol (if adrenal insufficiency suspected).
- **Imaging:**
  - • CXR — look for cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoidosis).
  - • Echo — structural heart disease, LV function, vegetations (endocarditis causing block).
- **Special tests:**
  - • Heart rate variability / tilt-table (if neurogenic syncope suspected after initial workup negative).
- **Diagnostic criteria (not formal scoring):**
  - • Symptomatic bradycardia: HR <50-60 with any of hypotension (SBP<90), syncope, presyncope, chest pain, heart failure, confusion.
  - • Sick sinus: documented bradycardia (<40) or pause >3 seconds on monitoring.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **12-lead ECG** — the single investigation that identifies rhythm abnormality; omission leads to missed CHB or MI.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - • ABC approach. Ensure IV access x2.
  - • If unstable (SBP <90, altered consciousness, chest pain, ischaemic ECG, heart failure): **perform transcutaneous pacing** (TCP) — place pads anterior-posterior. Set rate 60-80 bpm. Start at 50mA, increase until capture.
  - • While preparing for pacing: give **atropine** 500 mcg IV bolus. May repeat every 3-5 min to max 3 mg (total). Not effective in low flow states or CHB (especially wide QRS).
  - • If no response to atropine: **isoprenaline** infusion (1 mg in 500 mL 5% dextrose, titrate to HR 50-60). [Check local policy for exact dose and availability]
  - • If pacing fails or unavailable: **external pacing pads** — call senior and cardiology for transvenous pacing (TVP).
  - • Treat reversible cause: hold offending drugs, give calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia, warm if hypothermic.
  - • Provide oxygen only if SpO2 <94%.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - • Admit to CCU / telemetry bed.
  - • Continuous ECG monitoring, record rhythm strips.
  - • Identify and treat underlying cause: ischaemia (ACS protocol), drugs (reduce/stop), electrolytes (correct), thyroid (thyroxine).
  - • Consult cardiology for consideration of permanent pacemaker if indicated (symptomatic sinus node disease, high-grade AV block).
  - • If temporary wire placed: ensure daily ECG, check pacing threshold, secure insertion site.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - • If no reversible cause: proceed to permanent pacemaker (PPM) insertion.
  - • If reversible (e.g., drug toxicity, hypothermia, Lyme disease) — manage cause and repeat ECG in 6 weeks; if recurrence, consider PPM.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - • IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 L over 4-6 hours if hypotensive and not fluid overloaded. Adjust for heart failure.
  - • VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily + anti-embolism stockings (unless active bleed or coagulopathy).
  - • Diet: as tolerated — no restrictions unless NBM for TVP.
  - • Observations: NEWS2 every 30 minutes until stable, then hourly for 24 hours, then 4-hourly.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Atropine** (first-line for acute symptomatic bradycardia):
  - • Dose: 500 mcg IV bolus over 1-2 minutes. Repeat every 3-5 min to max 3 mg.
  - • Avoid in: hypothermia, heart transplant (may cause paradoxical bradycardia).
  - • **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving >3 mg total — risk of anticholinergic toxicity (tachycardia, delirium, urinary retention). Always document cumulative dose.
- **Isoprenaline IV infusion:**
  - • Dose: 1 mg in 500 mL 5% dextrose (2 mcg/mL). Start at 0.5-1 mcg/min, titrate to HR 50-60. [Check local policy for exact concentration and infusion rate]
  - • Contraindications: severe hypertension, ventricular arrhythmias, digoxin toxicity.
  - • **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** no specific adjustment, but caution in hepatic impairment — increased risk of arrhythmia.
  - • **Most dangerous dose error:** Over-administration causing ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation. Only use under continuous cardiac monitoring.
- **Adrenaline infusion** (if refractory to isoprenaline or asystole imminent):
  - • Dose: 1 mg in 500 mL (= 2 mcg/mL). Start at 2-10 mcg/min IV. [Check local policy]
- **Transcutaneous pacing** (not a drug — but ‘dose’ is current in mA):
  - • Set rate 60-80 bpm. Increase current until capture visible (usually 50-100 mA). Maintain 5-10 mA above capture threshold.

**Key contraindications / interactions:**
- • Atropine: avoid in glaucoma, obstructive uropathy, myasthenia gravis, toxic megacolon.
- • Isoprenaline: avoid in acute MI (may worsen ischaemia), hyperthyroidism.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - • Asystole / ventricular standstill — if pacing delayed
  - • Hypotension / cardiogenic shock
  - • Pulmonary oedema (from fluid overload)
  - • Complications of pacing: tamponade (rare with temporary wire), infection, pneumothorax (central line insertion)
- **Chronic:**
  - • Heart failure (due to low cardiac output)
  - • Pacemaker complications: lead fracture, pocket infection, battery failure
sections.Prognosis:
- • Reversible causes (drugs, hypothermia, infection) — good with treatment. Unreversible high-grade AV block — permanent pacing restores near-normal survival but depends on underlying heart disease.
- • 1-year mortality after symptomatic bradycardia untreated (especially CHB) >30%; with pacing <10% in structurally normal heart.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- • **Always perform a 12-lead ECG immediately** — it differentiates sinus bradycardia from heart block, and identifies ischaemic changes (inferior MI can cause CHB).
- • **Check drug history** — especially beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, amiodarone. Hold them immediately if bradycardia is symptomatic.
- • **Pre-emptively attach pacing pads** to any patient with symptomatic bradycardia, even if currently stable — can save time if deterioration occurs.
- • **Consider IV magnesium** 2 g over 10 min if bradycardia due to digoxin toxicity or hypomagnesaemia (especially if prolonged QT).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- • **Do not give atropine for Mobitz II or CHB with wide QRS** — it rarely works and may worsen block by increasing atrial rate without improving conduction.
- • **Do not delay pacing for prolonged ‘trial’ of medical therapy** in unstable patients — TCP should be initiated immediately if SBP <90 or patient unconscious.
- • **Do not assume asymptomatic bradycardia is benign** — if HR <40 without symptoms, still investigate for sick sinus or high-grade block; symptoms may be subtle (confusion, falls).
- • **Do not use external pacing without confirming electrical capture** — always check ECG for QRS complex after each stimulus (muscle twitching does not mean capture).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - • Any recurrence of syncope, near-syncope, dizziness, or palpitations
  - • Chest pain or shortness of breath
  - • Heart rate drops below 50 bpm at rest (if previously higher)
- **Follow-up:**
  - • Cardiology outpatient within 2 weeks (sooner if pending PPM)
  - • 24-hour Holter monitor (if outpatient) or implantable loop recorder for infrequent symptoms
  - • Repeat TSH if hypothyroidism found
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior reg / CCU immediately if:**
  - • Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid challenge
  - • Heart rate <40 bpm
  - • Altered consciousness (GCS <15)
  - • ANY episode of asystole or ventricular arrhythmia
  - • Failure to capture with TCP after 10 minutes
  - • NEWS2 ≥5
  - • Lactate >4 mmol/L (suggests hypoperfusion)
- **Call crash team for:**
  - • Unresponsive patient with bradycardia
  - • SBP <80 mmHg and not improving
  - • Cardiac arrest

rawMarkdown:
## Bradycardia — Symptomatic
**One-liner:** HR <50-60 with hypotension, syncope, or pre-syncope — risk of asystole; common in sick sinus, heart block, drug toxicity, or acute MI.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Complete heart block (CHB) with escape rhythm failure:** wide QRS + ventricular rate <30 + hypotension → imminent arrest. **Finding:** P-waves not related to QRS, slow wide-complex rhythm.
- **Sinus arrest / ventricular standstill:** prolonged pause (>3 seconds) on monitor → syncope or seizure. **Finding:** flatline on ECG strip with no escape beat for >3 seconds.

### Presentation
- • Dizziness, presyncope, near-syncope, or syncope (often with exertion)
- • Fatigue, exertional dyspnoea, confusion (in elderly)
- • Chest pain (if ischaemic cause)
- • Palpitations (if bradycardia with underlying tachycardia — ‘tachy-brady syndrome’)

**Key exam findings:**
- • Slow radial pulse (usually <50 bpm if symptomatic)
- • Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) — sign of haemodynamic instability
- • Cool peripheries, reduced cap refill, oliguria
- • Signs of heart failure: raised JVP, peripheral oedema, basal crackles
- • Carotid bruit (if vascular cause)
- • Assess for signs of hypothyroidism: coarse skin, delayed reflexes, gotre

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sinus bradycardia (physiological or pathological):** regular rhythm, P-wave before each QRS, often rate 40-60. Common in athletes, young adults.
- **First-degree AV block:** PR interval >200ms. Usually asymptomatic — rarely cause of syncope.
- **Second-degree AV block Mobitz I (Wenckebach):** progressive PR lengthening then dropped QRS. Usually benign unless very slow.
- **Second-degree AV block Mobitz II:** constant PR, then dropped QRS — risk of progression to CHB. Always consider ischaemia.
- **Complete heart block:** P-waves marching through, QRS independently slow (narrow or wide). Requires monitoring for pacing.
- **Sick sinus syndrome:** alternating bradycardia (often <40) with episodes of atrial tachycardia; pause after termination of tachycardia.
- **Drug-induced:** beta-blockers, verapamil/diltiazem, digoxin, amiodarone, lithium, clonidine, methyldopa.
- **Hypothyroidism:** bradycardia + cold intolerance + weight gain + dry skin.
- **Hypothermia:** core temp <35°C + bradycardia (Osborn J waves on ECG).
- **Raised intracranial pressure:** Cushing reflex (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing).

**Splitter:** Narrow QRS vs wide QRS escape rhythm in bradycardia. Wide QRS (especially >120ms) suggests infranodal block (Mobitz II, CHB) — higher risk of asystole — needs urgent pacing. Narrow QRS suggests nodal disease (sinus node, AV node) — often more amenable to atropine.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - • Immediate 12-lead ECG — document rhythm, PR interval, QRS width, ischaemic changes.
  - • Continuous cardiac monitoring — look for pauses, progression to CHB.
  - • Blood pressure every 5 minutes (if unstable).
  - • Capillary glucose — exclude hypoglycaemia as cause of collapse.
- **Bloods:**
  - • Routine: FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, troponin, thyroid function (TSH), digoxin level (if on digoxin).
  - • Consider: drug screen (if suspected toxicity), cortisol (if adrenal insufficiency suspected).
- **Imaging:**
  - • CXR — look for cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoidosis).
  - • Echo — structural heart disease, LV function, vegetations (endocarditis causing block).
- **Special tests:**
  - • Heart rate variability / tilt-table (if neurogenic syncope suspected after initial workup negative).
- **Diagnostic criteria (not formal scoring):**
  - • Symptomatic bradycardia: HR <50-60 with any of hypotension (SBP<90), syncope, presyncope, chest pain, heart failure, confusion.
  - • Sick sinus: documented bradycardia (<40) or pause >3 seconds on monitoring.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **12-lead ECG** — the single investigation that identifies rhythm abnormality; omission leads to missed CHB or MI.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - • ABC approach. Ensure IV access x2.
  - • If unstable (SBP <90, altered consciousness, chest pain, ischaemic ECG, heart failure): **perform transcutaneous pacing** (TCP) — place pads anterior-posterior. Set rate 60-80 bpm. Start at 50mA, increase until capture.
  - • While preparing for pacing: give **atropine** 500 mcg IV bolus. May repeat every 3-5 min to max 3 mg (total). Not effective in low flow states or CHB (especially wide QRS).
  - • If no response to atropine: **isoprenaline** infusion (1 mg in 500 mL 5% dextrose, titrate to HR 50-60). [Check local policy for exact dose and availability]
  - • If pacing fails or unavailable: **external pacing pads** — call senior and cardiology for transvenous pacing (TVP).
  - • Treat reversible cause: hold offending drugs, give calcium gluconate for hyperkalaemia, warm if hypothermic.
  - • Provide oxygen only if SpO2 <94%.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - • Admit to CCU / telemetry bed.
  - • Continuous ECG monitoring, record rhythm strips.
  - • Identify and treat underlying cause: ischaemia (ACS protocol), drugs (reduce/stop), electrolytes (correct), thyroid (thyroxine).
  - • Consult cardiology for consideration of permanent pacemaker if indicated (symptomatic sinus node disease, high-grade AV block).
  - • If temporary wire placed: ensure daily ECG, check pacing threshold, secure insertion site.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - • If no reversible cause: proceed to permanent pacemaker (PPM) insertion.
  - • If reversible (e.g., drug toxicity, hypothermia, Lyme disease) — manage cause and repeat ECG in 6 weeks; if recurrence, consider PPM.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - • IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1 L over 4-6 hours if hypotensive and not fluid overloaded. Adjust for heart failure.
  - • VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily + anti-embolism stockings (unless active bleed or coagulopathy).
  - • Diet: as tolerated — no restrictions unless NBM for TVP.
  - • Observations: NEWS2 every 30 minutes until stable, then hourly for 24 hours, then 4-hourly.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Atropine** (first-line for acute symptomatic bradycardia):
  - • Dose: 500 mcg IV bolus over 1-2 minutes. Repeat every 3-5 min to max 3 mg.
  - • Avoid in: hypothermia, heart transplant (may cause paradoxical bradycardia).
  - • **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving >3 mg total — risk of anticholinergic toxicity (tachycardia, delirium, urinary retention). Always document cumulative dose.
- **Isoprenaline IV infusion:**
  - • Dose: 1 mg in 500 mL 5% dextrose (2 mcg/mL). Start at 0.5-1 mcg/min, titrate to HR 50-60. [Check local policy for exact concentration and infusion rate]
  - • Contraindications: severe hypertension, ventricular arrhythmias, digoxin toxicity.
  - • **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** no specific adjustment, but caution in hepatic impairment — increased risk of arrhythmia.
  - • **Most dangerous dose error:** Over-administration causing ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation. Only use under continuous cardiac monitoring.
- **Adrenaline infusion** (if refractory to isoprenaline or asystole imminent):
  - • Dose: 1 mg in 500 mL (= 2 mcg/mL). Start at 2-10 mcg/min IV. [Check local policy]
- **Transcutaneous pacing** (not a drug — but ‘dose’ is current in mA):
  - • Set rate 60-80 bpm. Increase current until capture visible (usually 50-100 mA). Maintain 5-10 mA above capture threshold.

**Key contraindications / interactions:**
- • Atropine: avoid in glaucoma, obstructive uropathy, myasthenia gravis, toxic megacolon.
- • Isoprenaline: avoid in acute MI (may worsen ischaemia), hyperthyroidism.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - • Asystole / ventricular standstill — if pacing delayed
  - • Hypotension / cardiogenic shock
  - • Pulmonary oedema (from fluid overload)
  - • Complications of pacing: tamponade (rare with temporary wire), infection, pneumothorax (central line insertion)
- **Chronic:**
  - • Heart failure (due to low cardiac output)
  - • Pacemaker complications: lead fracture, pocket infection, battery failure

### Prognosis
- • Reversible causes (drugs, hypothermia, infection) — good with treatment. Unreversible high-grade AV block — permanent pacing restores near-normal survival but depends on underlying heart disease.
- • 1-year mortality after symptomatic bradycardia untreated (especially CHB) >30%; with pacing <10% in structurally normal heart.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- • **Always perform a 12-lead ECG immediately** — it differentiates sinus bradycardia from heart block, and identifies ischaemic changes (inferior MI can cause CHB).
- • **Check drug history** — especially beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin, amiodarone. Hold them immediately if bradycardia is symptomatic.
- • **Pre-emptively attach pacing pads** to any patient with symptomatic bradycardia, even if currently stable — can save time if deterioration occurs.
- • **Consider IV magnesium** 2 g over 10 min if bradycardia due to digoxin toxicity or hypomagnesaemia (especially if prolonged QT).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- • **Do not give atropine for Mobitz II or CHB with wide QRS** — it rarely works and may worsen block by increasing atrial rate without improving conduction.
- • **Do not delay pacing for prolonged ‘trial’ of medical therapy** in unstable patients — TCP should be initiated immediately if SBP <90 or patient unconscious.
- • **Do not assume asymptomatic bradycardia is benign** — if HR <40 without symptoms, still investigate for sick sinus or high-grade block; symptoms may be subtle (confusion, falls).
- • **Do not use external pacing without confirming electrical capture** — always check ECG for QRS complex after each stimulus (muscle twitching does not mean capture).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - • Any recurrence of syncope, near-syncope, dizziness, or palpitations
  - • Chest pain or shortness of breath
  - • Heart rate drops below 50 bpm at rest (if previously higher)
- **Follow-up:**
  - • Cardiology outpatient within 2 weeks (sooner if pending PPM)
  - • 24-hour Holter monitor (if outpatient) or implantable loop recorder for infrequent symptoms
  - • Repeat TSH if hypothyroidism found

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior reg / CCU immediately if:**
  - • Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid challenge
  - • Heart rate <40 bpm
  - • Altered consciousness (GCS <15)
  - • ANY episode of asystole or ventricular arrhythmia
  - • Failure to capture with TCP after 10 minutes
  - • NEWS2 ≥5
  - • Lactate >4 mmol/L (suggests hypoperfusion)
- **Call crash team for:**
  - • Unresponsive patient with bradycardia
  - • SBP <80 mmHg and not improving
  - • Cardiac arrest
